saida_bruta:
As the boys came up the hill, she saw them pause and confer. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You’d better, George. She visits your house often, and she barely knows me to speak to.”

As they halted before the three steps to the front porch, Mrs. Forrester appeared at the door and nodded graciously, a pink rose in her hand.

“Good morning, boys. Off for a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly removed his big straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we please fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. You have a lovely day. How long has school been out? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is very studious.”

The boys laughed, and Niel looked unhappy.

“Run along, and be sure not to leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester hates having the cattle on his bluegrass.”

The boys went quietly around the house to the grove gate, then ran shouting down the grassy slopes under the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched them from the kitchen window until they vanished behind the hill. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, when you bake this morning, put in a pan of cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down while they have their lunch.”

When they halted at the three front-porch steps, Mrs. Forrester stood in the doorway, smiling warmly and holding a pink rose.

“Good morning, boys. Off on a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly lifted his large straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. It’s a lovely day. How long has school been out? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is quite studious.”

The boys laughed, though Niel looked uneasy.

“Run along, and don’t leave the pasture gate open. Mr. Forrester hates cattle on his bluegrass.”

The boys walked quietly around the house to the grove gate, then raced shouting down the grassy slopes under the tall trees. Mrs. Forrester watched from the kitchen window until they vanished behind the hill. She turned to her Bohemian cook.

“Mary, when you bake this morning, make a pan of cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime.”
saida_filtrada:
As the boys climbed the hill, she watched them pause to talk. “You ask her, Niel.”

“You’d better, George. She comes to your house often, and she hardly knows me.”

When they halted at the three front-porch steps, Mrs. Forrester stood in the doorway, smiling warmly and holding a pink rose.

“Good morning, boys. Off on a picnic?”

George Adams stepped forward and solemnly lifted his large straw hat. “Good morning, Mrs. Forrester. May we fish and wade in the marsh and eat our lunch in the grove?”

“Certainly. It’s a lovely day. How long has school been out? Don’t you miss it? I’m sure Niel does. Judge Pommeroy says he is quite studious.”

The boys laughed, though Niel looked uneasy.

“Mary, when you bake this morning, make a pan of cookies for those boys. I’ll take them down at lunchtime.”
